〜〜〜
Costa Rica Histórica · Transport Series

Crossings

Five fords, five eras — men, machine, ox-team, and rail against moving water

Costa Rica's rivers have always demanded a toll of those who would pass. Before bridges reached the interior, every road met its match at the water's edge — and how a traveler crossed said as much about the age as where they were going.

This gathering sets five such moments side by side: a barefoot ford on the Atlantic slope, an oxcart team standing fast in a Pacific-side current, a motorcar nosing through a Guanacaste streambed, a hand-linked chain of travelers threading a forest stream, and a train inching across a storm-damaged trestle above the Reventazón. Separated by decades and by mode of travel, each is the same old negotiation between traveler and river.

〜 〜 〜
C-01
Magazine Plate · Atlantic Slope

Talamanca Indians Weighted Down With Heavy Stones to Aid in Maintaining Foothold in Crossing a Swiftly Flowing Stream

A page from The National Geographic Magazine, page 214, photograph credited to H. Wimmer. Three men ford a fast mountain stream single file, each shouldering a stone-weighted bundle — ballast carried deliberately to anchor their footing against the current rather than for any cargo purpose.

The accompanying caption observes that the country is threaded with countless rivers draining to both the Atlantic and Pacific, few of them navigable beyond their lower courses — a reminder that in the Talamanca highlands, fording was not a hazard of travel but its everyday condition.

Source
The National Geographic Magazine, p. 214
Photographer
H. Wimmer
Subject
Talamanca, Atlantic slope, Costa Rica
Medium
Half-tone magazine plate
The stone-as-ballast technique recorded here is a rare piece of practical ethnography — a method of crossing moving water that leaves no other trace in the documentary record.
C-02
Postcard · Vista del Campo

Vista del Campo, Costa Rica

Two ox-drawn carretas stand in mid-river, their teams braced against the current while three men ride and walk alongside. The lead cart's wheels are already submerged to the hub; the second team waits its turn on the bank behind. Dense gallery forest closes in on both sides of the watercourse.

The carreta was Costa Rica's working vehicle of the coffee and sugar economy for the better part of a century, and river crossings of exactly this kind — patient, deliberate, the ox-team's weight doing as much as its pull — were a routine feature of getting produce to market before all-weather roads reached the rural interior.

Title
"Vista del Campo, Costa Rica"
Format
Postcard
Subject
Oxcart river crossing, rural Costa Rica
Era
Early 20th century (carreta period)
Publisher and printer's mark not yet confirmed from this scan — worth revisiting against the verso if the physical card is at hand.
C-03
Photographic Print · Guanacaste

Between Bagares and Liberia

A streamlined sedan — rear-engined, with the swept, beetle-like profile characteristic of mid-century Tatra and Tatraplan touring cars — sits low in a wide, stony streambed somewhere on the road between Bagares and Liberia in Guanacaste. No bridge, no ford markers; the car simply drives the river bottom as the road.

The image carries a pastvu.com archive watermark (item 554569) tagged "TravelerCar," suggesting it circulated as a travel or motoring photograph rather than a studio postcard. The wide cobbled riverbed and dry-season water level point to Guanacaste's seasonal streams, passable by motorcar for a few months and an obstacle for the rest of the year.

Source
pastvu.com archive, item 554569
Archive tag
"TravelerCar"
Subject
Road between Bagares and Liberia, Guanacaste
Medium
Photographic print, halftone reproduction
Provenance beyond the archive watermark is unconfirmed — exact date and original photographer are not yet established. A useful counterpoint to C-01 and C-02: the same problem of moving water, solved this time by horsepower rather than human or ox power.
C-04
Photographic Print · Forest Stream Crossing

Hand in Hand Across the Current

Six travelers in Edwardian dress thread a shallow forest stream in a human chain, hands clasped shoulder to shoulder against the current's pull, while two more wait their turn on the boulders at either bank. A seated man at far left holds out a walking stick, perhaps offering a third point of balance to the woman stepping out ahead of him.

The dress — high-necked blouses, ankle-length skirts, straw boaters — and the unhurried, almost ceremonial posing point to an excursion rather than a journey of necessity: a country outing to a waterfall or swimming hole, the crossing itself treated as part of the day's adventure rather than an obstacle to be endured.

Source
Photographic print, personal archive
Subject
Forest stream crossing, Costa Rica
Period
Early 20th century (Edwardian dress)
Medium
Gelatin silver print
Unlike C-01's ballast technique or C-02's ox-team, this crossing relies on nothing but linked hands and shared balance — the simplest and oldest solution in the set, and the only one captured as leisure rather than labor.
C-05
Postcard Photograph · Cantón Carrillo, Guanacaste

Puente sobre el Río Tempisque, 1934

The camera stands at the centre of a steel Pratt through-truss bridge and looks back toward the Liberia approach, taking in the full portal frame and the diagonal bracing of the near span. The timber deck runs away beneath the lens; the far bank shows the dry-season canopy of the Guanacaste lowlands, stripped and pale. A single lane, no footway, the railing latticed in the Obras Públicas pattern of the period. The photograph was made to be sold: a commercial print, stamped Filadelfia, Guanacaste / Costa Rica, circulated as a record of infrastructure newly arrived.

The card caption is precise about both the engineering and the politics: “Puente sobre el río Tempisque construido en 1934 durante la Administración de Ricardo Jiménez.” Ricardo Jiménez Oreamuno was in his third and final term (1932–1936), and this bridge was the kind of public work that anchored a Guanacaste administration to the national project. The Tempisque had long been the principal barrier between Liberia and the canton of Carrillo; before the bridge, crossing was seasonal, dependent on water level and whatever improvised means were at hand. The 1934 span made Cantón Carrillo, and the Pacific coast beyond it, reachable on a schedule.

For anyone travelling today from Liberia toward Playa Hermosa and El Oasis, this is still the crossing that commits you to the coast — the moment the Liberia plain falls behind and Carrillo opens ahead. The bridge itself has been replaced, but the geometry of the passage remains: the Tempisque is where the drive changes character.

Source
Personal archive; commercial postcard photograph, Filadelfia, Guanacaste
Caption
“Puente sobre el río Tempisque construido en 1934 durante la Administración de Ricardo Jiménez. Es la entrada al Cantón Carrillo desde Liberia.”
Subject
Steel through-truss road bridge, Río Tempisque, Cantón Carrillo, Guanacaste; constructed 1934
Medium
Gelatin silver print; commercial postcard
The first purpose-built road span in this catalogue, and the only plate with a direct connection to the present: the Tempisque crossing remains the threshold between the Liberia plain and Cantón Carrillo. The bridge of 1934 is gone; the necessity of the crossing is not. See also C-06 for the contemporaneous failure of the Reventázón railway embankment — infrastructure built in the same decade, on the opposite coast, with a different outcome.
C-06
ANCR Photograph · Reventázón Valley, Atlantic Slope

Between Peralta and Torito, After the Flood of 1949

A figure in straw hat and dark work clothes stands at the broken edge of the Atlantic Railway right-of-way, looking out over the water that has replaced it. The rails and ties run forward from the foreground and end abruptly where the ballast has been scoured away; beyond that point there is nothing but brown, turbid current from the overflowed Reventázón. A second figure is visible far to the right, dwarfed by the scale of the breach. Dense riverine vegetation closes the far bank.

The Peralta–Torito sector of the Northern Railway was one of the most flood-vulnerable stretches on the entire Atlantic route. The Reventázón, which had carved the pass the engineers followed, could reclaim that pass in a single night. The flood of 1949 was among the most destructive on record for this line, closing service along a corridor that carried both banana freight and the passenger trade linking the Central Valley with Limón. The photograph documents the immediate aftermath: track still in place where the embankment held, and then nothing where it didn’t.

Every other plate in this catalogue records someone or something making a crossing. This one records the crossing unmade — the moment when the river asserts its prior claim to the valley floor it carved, and the engineer’s embankment is returned to sediment.

Source
Archivo Nacional de Costa Rica (ANCR), CR-AN-AH-FO-004871
Caption
“Fotografía tomada después de la inundación de 1949 entre Peralta y Torito por el rebalse del río Reventázón”
Subject
Northern Railway (Ferrocarril al Atlántico), Peralta–Torito sector; flood damage; Reventázón Valley, 1949
Medium
Gelatin silver print; archival scan
The only plate in this catalogue where the crossing is absent rather than present — the river’s answer to the engineer’s trestle. For the companion railway context, see the De Limón a San José and Reventázón y la Línea a Limón series (R-series) in Costa Rica Histórica.
Costa Rica Histórica — Crossings · Compiled from the personal archive
